claimed_contribution: The paper provides a retrospective classification and frequency breakdown of JMR manuscript types for 1989-1991.
It identifies highly influential articles from the period using Google Scholar citation counts and an appendix of papers with at least 250 citations.
It offers an editor-level assessment that JMR reestablished breadth across consumer and quantitative domains during this period.
context: The paper analyzes Journal of Marketing Research content during 1989-1991, a period when JMR competed with Journal of Consumer Research and Marketing Science for top marketing manuscripts. The focus is the journal's topic-method composition and influence in academic marketing.
design_and_data: Houston created a rough categorization of all JMR articles published during 1989-1991 and reported category frequencies. Categories include consumer behavior, statistical modeling, data collection/measurement, branding, strategy, pricing, promotion/advertising, channels/organizational buying, and sales/sales force management. He also checked article citation counts using Google Scholar and listed in an appendix articles with at least 250 citations. The analysis is descriptive and retrospective.
main_findings: JMR published a diverse portfolio of substantive and methodological work in 1989-1991, with statistical modeling accounting for about 29-30% of articles.
Consumer research was prevalent and, in Houston's assessment, JMR regained status as an important outlet for consumer behavior while retaining strength in quantitative manuscripts.
Articles in this period helped establish influential perspectives, including categorization theory in brand and brand extension research and transaction cost analysis in channel relationships.
one_line_summary: This editorial retrospectively classifies JMR articles from 1989-1991 and their citation impact to assess whether the journal regained breadth in consumer and quantitative research under Houston's editorship.
research_gap: By 1989, JCR and Marketing Science had become major outlets, and Houston perceived that JMR's position for consumer behavior and quantitative manuscripts may have weakened. The paper addresses this concern by documenting publication mix and influential articles from 1989-1991.
research_question: During Houston's 1989-1991 editorship, what mix of manuscript types appeared in JMR, and which articles became most influential? Did JMR reestablish itself as a key outlet for consumer behavior while maintaining strength in quantitative and methodological work?
